items.q: How do vaccines protect you from a disease?
items.correct: They expose your immune system to a harmless form of the pathogen so memory cells form, allowing rapid response if exposed later.
items.misconception: Vaccines kill the pathogen if it ever enters the body.
items.random_wrong: Vaccines provide antibodies for life by adding them directly to the blood.
items.why: Students think vaccines actively fight pathogens themselves, rather than priming the immune system to fight.

items.q: What is the key structural difference between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells?
items.correct: Eukaryotes have a membrane-bound nucleus and membrane-bound organelles; prokaryotes do not.
items.misconception: Prokaryotes have no DNA — that's why they have no nucleus.
items.random_wrong: Prokaryotes are always smaller than 1 micrometre and have no cytoplasm.
items.why: Students see 'no nucleus' and think 'no DNA', missing that prokaryotes still have DNA — it's just free in the cytoplasm.

items.correct: They are random changes in DNA — caused by errors in replication or by mutagens — not directed by need.
items.misconception: Mutations occur because organisms need to adapt to their environment.
items.random_wrong: Mutations only happen in organisms that have been damaged by external chemicals.
items.why: Students confuse mutation with adaptation and think evolution involves organisms 'trying' to change — a Lamarckian rather than Darwinian view.

items.q: Why don't antibiotics work on viruses?
items.correct: Viruses live inside your own cells
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Viruses live inside your own cells. “Viruses are too big” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's A dead or weakened pathogen. “A live, full-strength pathogen” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: 1 bacterium doubles every 20 minutes. How many bacteria are there after 2 hours?
items.why: The real figure is 64. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
